text:
Overview
Kubernetes automates deployment, scaling, and operations of containerized applications
across a cluster of nodes. Understanding its control-plane/data-plane split and core API
objects is foundational to securing managed offerings like AKS, EKS, and GKE.
Control Plane Components
Component
Role
API Server
Front door for all cluster operations; authenticates and validates
requests
etcd
Distributed key-value store holding all cluster state — highest-value
target to protect
Scheduler
Assigns pods to nodes based on resource requests, affinity, taints/
tolerations
Controller Manager
Runs reconciliation loops (ReplicaSet, Node, Endpoint controllers)
toward desired state
kubelet
Node agent; ensures containers described in PodSpecs are running
and healthy
kube-proxy
Maintains network rules enabling Service abstraction and load
balancing
Core Workload Objects
Pod — smallest deployable unit; one or more co-located containers sharing network/
storage

kube-proxy
Maintains network rules enabling Service abstraction and load
balancing
Core Workload Objects
Pod — smallest deployable unit; one or more co-located containers sharing network/
storage
Deployment — manages ReplicaSets for stateless apps; supports rolling updates and
rollback
StatefulSet — stable network identity and persistent storage per replica, for stateful
workloads
C L O U D C H E AT S H E E T · C O N TA I N E R S
Kubernetes
Container orchestration architecture, workload objects, and security hardening
• 
• 
• 
Mazhar Khan — Cloud Security Portfolio
Page 1 of 4

DaemonSet — ensures one pod per node (log shippers, node-level security agents)
Job / CronJob — run-to-completion and scheduled batch workloads
Service — stable virtual IP/DNS abstracting a dynamic set of pod endpoints (ClusterIP/
NodePort/LoadBalancer)
Ingress — L7 HTTP(S) routing into the cluster, typically backed by an Ingress Controller
Security Hardening Checklist
RBAC — least-privilege Roles/ClusterRoles bound via RoleBindings; avoid cluster-admin
for workloads
Pod Security Standards — enforce baseline/restricted profiles (replaces deprecated
PodSecurityPolicy)
Network Policies — default-deny ingress/egress, explicitly allow required pod-to-pod
traffic
Secrets management — avoid plain Secrets objects for high-value data; integrate
external secret stores (Key Vault CSI driver)
Image security — scan images in CI, enforce signed/trusted registries via admission
control
Admission Controllers — OPA Gatekeeper / Kyverno to enforce policy-as-code at
admission time

Image security — scan images in CI, enforce signed/trusted registries via admission
control
Admission Controllers — OPA Gatekeeper / Kyverno to enforce policy-as-code at
admission time
Workload identity — bind pod service accounts to cloud IAM roles instead of
embedding credentials
Managed Kubernetes Comparison
Service
Notable Traits
AKS (Azure)
Free control plane; integrates with Entra ID RBAC, Azure Policy,
Defender for Containers
EKS (AWS)
IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA), tight VPC CNI integration
GKE (GCP)
Autopilot mode abstracts node management; strong default security
posture
kubectl Essentials
Command
Purpose
kubectl get pods -n ns
List pods in a namespace

kubectl describe pod x
Inspect events, status, resource config
kubectl logs -f pod
Stream container logs
kubectl exec -it pod --
sh
Interactive shell into a container
kubectl apply -f
file.yaml
Declarative create/update from manifest
kubectl rollout undo
deploy/x
Roll back a deployment
Common Pitfalls
Pitfall
Fix
Running workloads
as root with no
security context
Set runAsNonRoot, drop capabilities, enforce restricted Pod Security
Standard
Secrets stored
unencrypted in etcd
Enable encryption at rest for etcd; consider external secret stores
No resource
requests/limits set
Define requests/limits on every container to avoid noisy-neighbor
and eviction issues
Flat cluster network,
no NetworkPolicy
Default-deny and explicitly allow required pod-to-pod communication
Cluster-admin bound
to CI/CD service
account
Scope CI/CD RBAC tightly to the namespaces and verbs actually
required
Interview / Exam Quick Hits

Cluster-admin bound
to CI/CD service
account
Scope CI/CD RBAC tightly to the namespaces and verbs actually
required
Interview / Exam Quick Hits
etcd should be encrypted at rest and access restricted — it holds Secrets in plaintext
by default
Liveness probes restart unhealthy containers; readiness probes control traffic admission,
not restarts
Namespaces provide logical isolation, not a hard security boundary — combine with
NetworkPolicy + RBAC
HPA scales pod replicas on metrics; VPA adjusts resource requests/limits; Cluster
Autoscaler adds/removes nodes

• 
• 
•

Page 3 of 4

Taints repel pods from a node; tolerations allow specific pods to be scheduled there
anyway
